now it's at a weak positive week

trend. Uh we're not talking about the
six dynamics in productivity growth.
We're talking about the structural
dynamics in productivity growth. Right
now the US has been a 2% productivity
growth economy on a trend basis. Looking
at the 2015 to 2019 trend, the promise
of AI is that productivity goes to 3% on
a trend basis and unit labor costs goes
to 1% on a trend basis. They're
currently both around 2%. That's a
massive change in terms of how the
economy operates, particularly from the
perspective of un of inflation. A
productivity boom is inherently
disinflationary and would allow the Fed
to retain credibility regarding its
arbitrary 2% inflation target whilst
easing amid sticky inflation. So again

going to see inflation start to
decelerate on a trend basis as well.

investment deficit GDP ratio of the US
at minus 87%. It's like the most by far
the most absurd net international
investment deficit in the history of
mankind. Foreigners own 90% of our GDP's
worth on a net basis of US assets. Never
seen anything like it. No other economy
has ever come close. Uh if you look at
population,
US is 4% of the global population, 25%
of global GDP. Yet we're 30% of the
global bond market, 49% of global
stocks, 60% of global FX reserves in
terms of the dollar, 60% of crossber
bank lending, 70% of national debt
securities, 79% of trade invoicing, 88%
foreign chain transactions, and 99% of
stable coin backing. I got to tell you
right now, if we had a dual erosion of
US central bank independence,

volatility are countable leading
indicators of global liquidity accur
according to our global liquidity proxy.

asset markets according to our global
liquidity proxy as well. Global
liquidity has consolidated recently as
evidenced by the minus 2% uh month over
month annualized rate of change
contraction in our global liquidity
proxy and may continue consolidating for
months. Again, if the Fed is sloted with
regards to, you know, supplying the arm
up, then global liquidity continue
contracting because the dollar is going
to continue gradually increasing on a
trend basis. However, if we're right on
structural reform at the Fed, global
liquidity will unexpectedly
unconsolidate higher, not lower. I know
the consensus in the crypto community is
that liquidity cycle starting to peak
and roll over, and that is probably true
as evidenced by the data. But if the
next Fed chair comes in and is
successful relative to market current
market expectations of implementing
structural reforms in a way that pivots
the institution to a more progrowth
policy bias which will obviously require
more doubbish monetary policy, then
you're talking about a dollar that might
just make a new low out of nowhere. And
a dollar that makes a new low out of
nowhere is liquidity that makes a new
high out of nowhere. And asset prices
that make a new high out of nowhere. So
I want investors to be aware of that
risk. Not saying it's the highest
probability out in our opinion, this is
the modal outcome.
I don't know that the modal outcome is a
very, you know, got a lot of excess
curtosis. It's probably pretty pretty
flat distribution, but in our opinion,
we think structural reform at the Fed is
the modal outcome. And so investors need
to be aware that, hey, everyone thinks
the liquidity cycle is starting to roll
over. I'm telling you right now that if
we're right on structural form of the
Fed, and that's a big if,
cycle will consolidate, maybe even roll
over a little bit, but then it'll
explode higher at some point in the
second half of next year. So just be
aware of that outcome, potential
outcome.
